| Safety and Reliability | Risks often arise from brittle logic, missing rules, or edge-case failure | Risks often arise from hallucination, prompt injection, stochastic instability, or tool misuse | Safety engineering must be paradigm-specific rather than generic |
| Human Oversight | Oversight can focus on rule correctness and state interpretation | Oversight often requires monitoring context, prompts, outputs, and orchestration | Human supervision must be tailored to the form of agency being governed |
| Hybrid Complexity | Less relevant in pure symbolic systems | Less relevant in pure neural systems | Hybrid systems inherit both symbolic and neural governance burdens |

: Paradigm-Specific Ethical and Governance Challenges in Agentic AI {#tbl-ethical_challenges}

A major conclusion follows from this comparison: **effective governance cannot be architecturally neutral**. It is not enough to say that all AI systems should be explainable, fair, safe, or accountable in the same way. Those values remain important, but they must be operationalized differently depending on how the system actually works. For a symbolic system, governance may focus on validating logical rules, checking constraints, and ensuring that the internal representation is consistent and safe. For a neural system, the governance burden shifts toward training data, model behavior, prompt pathways, retrieval sources, tool access, and output auditing.

This becomes especially difficult in hybrid systems. A neuro-symbolic architecture may require one layer of oversight to verify symbolic logic and another to monitor generative or stochastic behavior. That makes hybrid systems promising, but it also makes them harder to govern responsibly. Their strength lies in combining complementary capabilities; their governance challenge lies in combining complementary risks.

A particularly difficult issue in the neural paradigm is the **attribution gap**. When a neural agent makes a harmful recommendation, misuses a tool, or produces a faulty output, responsibility is not always easy to assign. The failure may depend on training data, prompt structure, orchestration design, retrieval context, tool execution, or user interaction. Traditional legal ideas such as direct causation and clear intentional action fit awkwardly in such systems. This is one reason why liability debates around neural agents remain unsettled.

Human oversight also changes across paradigms. Supervising a symbolic system is often similar to checking a formal decision process: the supervisor can examine rules, inspect states, and review plans. Supervising a neural system is more like managing an open-ended reasoning process whose internal mechanics are only partially visible. In such cases, oversight depends more heavily on bounding context, validating external evidence, restricting tools, and auditing outputs after the fact.

These differences show why broad, one-size-fits-all governance language is insufficient. Ethical and legal frameworks for agentic AI will need to become more architecture-aware if they are to remain meaningful.

## Toward Agentic AI Policy {#sec-ai_policy}

The governance problem also raises a policy problem. Existing AI policy discussions often focus on models that generate outputs, rank cases, or support decisions. Agentic AI adds another layer because these systems do not merely generate responses; they may initiate actions, coordinate tasks, call tools, and make choices over time. That means policy must account not only for model capability, but also for **degree of agency**.

A useful way to think about this is to distinguish systems by how much autonomy they exercise and what kind of human control remains in place. Some systems are best understood as assistive: they produce recommendations, drafts, or analytic support, but a human remains the active decision-maker. Others operate through shared agency: the human and system interact dynamically, with decision-making distributed across the partnership. Still others approach delegated agency: the system is granted authority to act within bounded domains with only limited human intervention.

Table @tbl-agency_policy summarizes these broad levels of agency and the different policy implications they raise.

| Level of Agency | General Characteristics | Typical Human Role | Policy and Governance Implications |
|---|---|---|---|
| Assistive Agency | Produces suggestions, analyses, drafts, or recommendations; does not independently execute major actions | Human remains primary decision-maker | Emphasis on transparency, disclosure, and decision support accountability |
| Shared Agency | System and human jointly shape decisions through iterative interaction | Human supervises and can intervene during execution | Emphasis on meaningful human control, traceability, and bounded autonomy |
| Delegated Agency | System is allowed to execute actions or workflows within defined constraints | Human oversight becomes supervisory rather than immediate | Stronger requirements for auditing, liability assignment, fail-safes, and domain-specific restrictions |

: A Simple Policy-Oriented Taxonomy of Agency in Agentic AI {#tbl-agency_policy}

This way of framing policy is useful because it connects governance to the **degree of autonomy actually exercised**. It also helps clarify why “meaningful human control” [@feng2025levels] cannot be treated as a single uniform requirement. What meaningful control looks like in an assistive writing tool is different from what it should mean in an autonomous research workflow or a semi-delegated enterprise agent. Policy therefore needs to scale with agency.

# Research Gaps {#sec-gaps}

The development of agentic AI is constrained by important unresolved problems, but those problems are not the same across paradigms. One of the weaknesses of current discussion is that it often identifies “gaps” in general terms without asking which architecture those gaps belong to. A more useful roadmap distinguishes between what remains underdeveloped in symbolic systems, what remains underdeveloped in neural systems, and where hybrid systems present new opportunities or new difficulties.

Table @tbl-research_gaps summarizes several of the major open problems in a paradigm-aware way.

| Research Theme | Symbolic / Classical Gap | Neural / Generative Gap | Hybrid / Integrative Opportunity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reasoning and Adaptability | Strong formal reasoning but limited flexibility in open environments | Flexible adaptation but weak guarantees of logical consistency | Neuro-symbolic systems may combine structured reasoning with adaptive perception and generation |
| Evaluation | Mature formal evaluation in narrow settings but limited scalability | Many benchmarks exist, but they often fail to capture long-horizon reliability or coordination quality | Need evaluation suites that can compare integrated systems without collapsing paradigm differences |
| Governance and Safety | Underexplored governance for modern symbolic complexity | Major open questions in alignment, robustness, prompt security, and accountability | Need mixed governance frameworks that can audit both rule-based and stochastic components |
| Memory and Continuity | Explicit state is possible but often brittle or difficult to maintain at scale | Many systems remain stateless or weak in long-term memory | Persistent memory architectures may benefit from combining symbolic structure with neural retrieval |
| Multi-Agent Coordination | Strong protocol design but limited flexibility | Flexible orchestration but weak guarantees | Hybrid coordination models may combine protocol reliability with adaptive collaboration |
| Benchmarking Methodology | Often too narrow or domain-specific | Often centered on neural tasks only | Need paradigm-aware benchmarks that test architecture-specific failure modes |

: Paradigm-Specific Research Gaps in Agentic AI {#tbl-research_gaps}

A major issue running through all of these gaps is the lack of **paradigm-aware research methodology**. Too often, tools developed for one class of systems are treated as if they were naturally appropriate for another. Formal symbolic guarantees do not tell us enough about stochastic neural systems. At the same time, neural-agent benchmarks built around prompt robustness or tool use do not necessarily say much about symbolic planners. This mismatch slows progress because it creates confusion about what success even means.

The most promising path forward is therefore not just deeper work inside each paradigm, but also better work at the boundary between them. The question is no longer only how to improve symbolic systems or how to improve neural systems separately. It is increasingly how to connect them in ways that preserve their strengths without simply combining their weaknesses.

Evaluation is especially urgent. The field needs more than general-purpose leaderboards. It needs benchmark suites that are sensitive to architecture. Symbolic systems should be stress-tested for logical failure, brittleness, and edge-case reasoning breakdown. Neural systems should be stress-tested for hallucination, prompt injection, context instability, and long-horizon drift. Hybrid systems should be evaluated for whether their integration layers genuinely improve reliability rather than merely adding complexity.

Governance is another area where research gaps remain substantial. Policymakers and ethicists increasingly recognize the need for AI oversight, but practical frameworks for agentic AI still lag behind technical development. Symbolic systems and neural systems require different audit strategies, and hybrid systems complicate both. Research on governance therefore cannot remain abstract. It must become architecture-aware in the same way that technical evaluation must.

Taken together, these gaps suggest that the future of the field depends on a dual-track strategy: one track deepens understanding within each paradigm, while the other develops the concepts, tools, and architectures needed to integrate them effectively.

# Future Directions {#sec-future}

The future of agentic AI is unlikely to be defined by one paradigm replacing the other. Instead, the field is moving toward deeper integration, broader coordination, and more deliberate forms of hybrid intelligence [@ma2025agentic]. This future will depend not only on better models, but on better ways of combining reasoning, generation, memory, and control.

Table @tbl-future_directions outlines several of the major trajectories now visible in the field.

| Future Direction | Symbolic / Classical Development | Neural / Generative Development | Hybrid Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reasoning Integration | More formal planning, verification, and rule consistency | Better long-context reasoning, planning prompts, and tool orchestration | Neuro-symbolic integration remains the clearest route toward reliable and adaptable systems |
| Multi-Agent Ecosystems | Structured coordination protocols and explicit constraints | LLM-based collaboration, dialogue, and dynamic orchestration | Future ecosystems are likely to mix symbolic and neural specialists within shared workflows |
| Governance and Safety | Continued development of verifiability and formal assurance | Better alignment, monitoring, red teaming, and output auditing | Governance of hybrid systems will require combining both traditions rather than choosing one |
| Memory and Lifelong Adaptation | Persistent explicit state and durable knowledge representation | Improved retrieval, long-term memory, and continual adaptation | Hybrid memory systems may become central to durable agentic performance |
| Integration with Other Technologies | Formal control over sensors, robotics, and structured environments | Real-time perception, multimodal generation, IoT and data-rich adaptation | Technological convergence will likely amplify the need for hybrid intelligence |

: Paradigm-Aware Future Directions in Agentic AI {#tbl-future_directions}

The dominant theme here is **architectural integration**. The most important advances are likely to come from systems that can combine symbolic reliability with neural flexibility. Neuro-symbolic integration is therefore not just one trend among many. It is increasingly the central idea around which future agentic research is being organized.

This will matter especially in multi-agent ecosystems [@huang2025agent; @bachrach2020negotiating]. Future systems are unlikely to consist only of homogeneous swarms of similar models. More likely, they will include multiple kinds of agents with different roles. Some may be highly neural, optimized for creativity, synthesis, or interaction with messy environments. Others may be highly symbolic, optimized for validation, regulation, constraint enforcement, or safety-critical reasoning. The challenge will be designing the protocols and orchestration logic that allow these heterogeneous agents to work together effectively.

Safety and governance will also need to evolve on two tracks at once [@gabison2025inherent]. Symbolic systems still benefit from formal methods, explicit verification, and logic-based auditing. Neural systems require stronger work in alignment, monitoring, adversarial testing, and statistical robustness. Hybrid systems will force these strands together, making it impossible to treat safety as if it belonged to only one side of the field.

The integration of agentic AI with other technological infrastructures will accelerate these pressures [@sultanow2025quantum; @radanliev2025rise]. Neural components will increasingly manage large streams of sensor data, multimodal content, and adaptive interactions. Symbolic components will remain important where decisions must satisfy compliance, safety, or formal constraints. In that sense, convergence with IoT, robotics, cloud systems, and even quantum research will not reduce the importance of paradigm distinctions. It will intensify them.

The long-term direction of the field, then, points toward **hybrid intelligence**: systems that are both adaptive and reliable, both creative and constrained, both exploratory and auditable.

# Conclusion {#sec-conclusion}

Agentic AI represents a major shift in how intelligent systems are designed and understood. But this shift has also produced conceptual confusion. Too often, modern systems are described with inherited language from earlier symbolic AI, as if today’s LLM-orchestrated agents were simply updated versions of classical planners. These notes have argued that this is misleading. The field is better understood as containing two distinct lineages: the **Symbolic/Classical** lineage and the **Neural/Generative** lineage.

That distinction matters because the two lineages do not merely differ in age or implementation detail. They differ in their core mechanics. Symbolic systems rely on explicit logic, formal state, and structured planning. Neural systems rely on statistical learning, stochastic generation, and orchestration over context, tools, and external resources. Treating them as if they were the same obscures both their strengths and their risks.

The review across applications, taxonomy, governance, and future directions leads to three major conclusions. First, the architectural divide is real and practically important. Symbolic systems remain especially useful in settings requiring safety, verifiability, and deterministic control, while neural systems are especially powerful in domains requiring adaptability, pattern recognition, and work over unstructured information. Second, governance cannot be one-size-fits-all. The ethical and legal questions raised by symbolic systems and neural systems differ too much for a single framework to capture adequately. Third, the most promising path forward is not strict competition between paradigms, but selective integration of both.

That is why hybrid systems now matter so much. The next important stage of agentic AI is likely to involve architectures that can combine symbolic reliability with neural adaptability without collapsing one into the other. Achieving that will require better evaluation methods, more architecture-aware governance, stronger coordination protocols, and more deliberate system design.

Ultimately, agentic AI is not only a technical development but a sociotechnical one. Its future depends on whether we can build systems that are not just capable, but trustworthy. That means designing agents that are not only powerful, but governable; not only flexible, but accountable; not only intelligent in performance, but sound in deployment. The long-term challenge is therefore not simply to make agents more capable. It is to make them reliable collaborators within the institutions, infrastructures, and domains in which they will operate.
